coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots promote the carrier recombination in Poly (9-vinyl carbazole) to enhance its electroluminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tianyang
Wang, Xiao
Wu, Zhenyu
Yang, Tianyu
Wang, Jiawei
Zhao, Han
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are one species of multifunctionally auxiliary materials with promising application in photoelectrocatalyst and photoelectrodevices. However, there are few reports on enhancing the electroluminescence (EL) of materials by CDs and the effects of CDs on the charge transport dynamics have not been revealed so far. Herein, CDs are firstly successful to be applied in the enhancement of EL of Poly (9-vinyl carbazole), which is set as model material for research, abbreviated as PVK with the EL wavelength of 432 nm. The brightness of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs reaches 176 cd m−2 that obviously 54.3 % higher than that of pure PVK based LEDs and other parameters are all improved. The Commission Internationale d’Eclairage coordinates of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs are situated at (0.17, 0.10). Notably, through the transient photovoltage technology and method of empirical mode decomposition, the mechanisms of charge transport dynamics between CDs and PVK molecules are determined. This work paves a new direction for the improvement of the luminescent properties of electroluminescent devices and a new mentality to understand the effects of CDs on charge transport in the EL process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Poly (9-vinyl carbazole)
Light-emitting diode
Charge transport
Improvement of electroluminescent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and Optical characterization of CDs
Performances characterizations of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs
Mechanism of CDs enhancing EL of PVK

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterizations of CDs. a) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image). b) FTIR spectrum. c) C1s XPS spectrum. d) XRD pattern.

Optical characterization of CDs. a) UV–vis (black line) and PL emission (red line) spectra. b) TRPL spectra. c) UPS spectra. d) The curve of (αhν)1/2 versus photon energy (hν). The blue and red dotted lines are acquired by tangent to the curves respectively. The intersection value reflects the information of the band gap. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The structures and surface topographies of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs and pure PVK based LEDs. a) Structural diagram of devices. b) Schematic diagram of energy level structures. c) The AFM image of pure PVK based LEDs. d) The AFM image of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs.

The EL performances of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs. a) EL spectra at the current density of 40 mA cm−2 with various CDs-PVK mass ratios from 0 to 4 wt%. b) EL spectra of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs under different current densities. c) EL spectra of pure PVK based LEDs under different current densities. d) EL spectra at the current density of 40 mA cm−2 with various CDs-PVK mass ratios of from 20 to 60 wt%. e) CIE coordinates of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs (inset: the photograph of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs). f) CIE coordinates of pure PVK based LEDs (inset: the photograph of pure PVK based LEDs).

The comparison diagrams of EL properties of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs (red lines) and pure PVK based LEDs (black lines). a) The curves power efficiency versus current density. b) The curves of current density versus voltage. c) The curves of luminance versus voltage. d) The curves of current efficiency versus current density. e) Half lifetime curves of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs (red line) and pure PVK based LEDs (black line). f) CIE coordinates of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs with various CDs-PVK mass ratios. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The PL lifetimes of CDIP with various mass fractions and detection principles and schematic diagrams of charge transport on PVK. a) The TRPL spectra of CDIP with various mass fractions. b) The spot and line image of PL lifetimes of CDIP with various mass ratios. c) The schematic diagram of TPV measurement principle. d) The schematic diagram of charge transport on PVK molecules after charge injection.

TPV measurements. a) TPV performance test of pure PVK. b) TPV performance test of 2 wt% CDIP. c) The signals of IMF1 of pure PVK and 2 wt% CDIP from EMD results. d) Periods distribution of signals in IMF1 of pure PVK and periods distribution of signals in IMF1 of 2 wt% CDIP. e) The signals of IMF3 of pure PVK and 2 wt% CDIP. f) The signals of IMF7 of pure PVK and 2 wt% CDIP.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots promote the carrier recombination in Poly (9-vinyl carbazole) to enhance its electroluminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215123, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa, Macau SAR 999078, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering
Macau University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa, Macau SAR 999078, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CDs are applied in the enhancement of EL of Poly (9-vinyl carbazole) with the increased brightness by 54.3 %.

Carbon dots (CDs) are one species of multifunctionally auxiliary materials with promising application in photoelectrocatalyst and photoelectrodevices. However, there are few reports on enhancing the electroluminescence (EL) of materials by CDs and the effects of CDs on the charge transport dynamics have not been revealed so far. Herein, CDs are firstly successful to be applied in the enhancement of EL of Poly (9-vinyl carbazole), which is set as model material for research, abbreviated as PVK with the EL wavelength of 432 nm. The brightness of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs reaches 176 cd m−2 that obviously 54.3 % higher than that of pure PVK based LEDs and other parameters are all improved. The Commission Internationale d’Eclairage coordinates of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs are situated at (0.17, 0.10). Notably, through the transient photovoltage technology and method of empirical mode decomposition, the mechanisms of charge transport dynamics between CDs and PVK molecules are determined. This work paves a new direction for the improvement of the luminescent properties of electroluminescent devices and a new mentality to understand the effects of CDs on charge transport in the EL process.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are late-model carbon nanoparticles with fluorescence properties while the sizes are all less than 10 nm.[1] CDs have attracted extensive attention in the recent years, since low cost, excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, environmental protection, stable fluorescence emission, high efficiency catalytic activity, excellent electron acceptor/donor characteristics and outstanding electron conductivity and electron reservoir characteristics.[2–16] Based on the above characteristics, CDs applied as functional materials to enhance performances have been broadly reported. The CDs can be combined with C3N4 to provide an alternative channel for the electron transport so that the complex materials can be used as a stable photocatalyst to split water with high efficiency.[17] Furthermore, CDs can be assembled on the WO3 nanosheets to form a novel Z-scheme nanosheets for the use of cyclohexane oxidation.[18] In addition, the power conversion efficiency of solar cells is improved by the addition of CDs that can enhance charge transport in functional layer materials and device stability.[19] Even more, CDs are able to be applied to enhance the capacities of Li-S batteries at high current densities by the method of doping in the sulfur cathodes.[20]

Light-emitting diode (LED) is one type of common light-emitting devices that release energy to realize electroluminescence (EL) through electrons recombining with holes, which has been widely used in the fields of lighting and display.[21] There are many factors that can affect the performances of devices but they can be summarized as follow: properties of materials, designs of device structures, charge transport inside materials and at the interfaces and so on.[22,23] CDs are excellent electron acceptors/donors and possess outstanding electron conductivity and electron reservoir characteristics, which have the potential to improve performances of EL devices.[21,24,25] However, there still have been few reports on the enhancement of EL by CDs nowadays.

Conducting polymer material (CPM) is a kind of polymeric materials based on covalent bonding with conductivity.[26] Poly (9-vinyl carbazole) (PVK) is a remarkable CPM as a result of widespread application, including modification and decoration of optoelectronic device structure, preparation of LEDs based on CPMs and photovoltaic devices and directly being used as photorefractive materials.[27] However, due to the poor conductivity, PVK can only be emerged as modified materials to enhance or assist the performances of other materials and devices instead of actively functional materials.[28–30] It must be admitted that PVK is not a good material for EL. In this work, we just take the PVK as model material for the research on the roles of which CDs play on the enhancement of EL of PVK.

Herein, we used an ordinary hydrothermal method with one step to firstly synthetize CDs with the properties of enhancing EL of CPMs. Then, the CDs were doped in PVK as an active emission layer for the manufacture of the CDs enhanced PVK LEDs. The CDs enhanced PVK LEDs own EL peaks of 432 nm possessing the brightness of 176 cd m−2, which is 54.3% larger than that of pure PVK based LEDs, and other performances of devices are all improved. The Commission Internationale d’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of the CDs enhanced PVK LEDs are situated at (0.17, 0.10). Furthermore, the transient photovoltage (TPV) tests and empirical mode decomposition (EMD) are firstly used to analyze the effects of CDs on the charge transport kinetic characteristics in the complex materials of CDs and PVK. This work provides a new methodology to enhance the luminescent properties of EL devices and supply a new mentality to understand the effects of CDs on charge transport during EL process.
All precursor and reagents were bought from Sinopharm Group with no purification.

The synthesis of the CDs with the properties of enhancing EL was carried out according to the reported method.[31] Specifically, 100 mg citric acid and 200 mg malonylurea were dispersed in the 15 mL deionized water. Afterwards 20 μL ethylenediamine was introduced into the precursor solution. After mixed homogeneously by the method of ultrasonic assisted dispersion, the precursor solution was diverted into a hydrothermal reaction vessel with the volume of 20 mL and react at 160℃ for 10 h. The mixed liquid was acquired at the end of the reaction after waiting until it cooled to room temperature. The silica gel column chromatography was applied to separate different components in the sample with dichloromethane and methanol as the eluent. Eventually, the rotary evaporator was used to evaporate the solvent to obtain the CDs powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and Optical characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are emerged in the (Fig. 1 a) to analyze the morphology of the CDs. (Fig. 1a) shows that the CDs have a splendid dispersion with nearly similar sizes. The crystal lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is clearly exhibited in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (inset in the Fig. 1a), which is assigned to the d-spacing of graphene (100) plane.[32] The size distribution of blue emission CDs is petty and uniform which is emerged in the Fig. S1
. Significantly, the CDs possess mean size of 1.96 nm by counting 102 particles. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra illustrated in the (Fig. 1b) show the stretching vibrations for N-H (3216 cm−1), C-H (2848 cm−1) and C = N/C = O/C = C (1572 cm−1) while bending vibration for C-C/C-N (1389 cm−1) and C-O (1265 cm−1).

Ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer is used to determine the absorbance of substances and then analyze the internal energy level transitions based on Lambert-Beer law. The survey spectrum of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) exhibited in the ( Fig. S2
a) displays three characteristic peaks, which are 531.1 eV, 399.8 eV and 285.1 eV respectively assigned to O, N, C elements. As shown in the (Fig. 1c), the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum implies three peaks situated at 284.6 eV, 286.0 eV and 287.6 eV, which are attributed to C-C/C = O, C-O/C-N and C = O separately. In the ( Fig. S2
b), the N 1 s XPS spectrum indicates one typical peak situated at 399.5 eV corresponding to pyridinic N while the other situated at 400.0 eV assigned to graphitic N. The O 1 s XPS spectrum shown in the ( Fig. S2
c) reveals the relative contributions of C = O and C-O signals which are separately centered at 530.9 eV and 531.7 eV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern is applied to analyze the composition and structure of materials. The XRD pattern of blue emission CDs is illustrated in the (Fig. 1d), which exhibits a broad peak situated at around 26° revealing the crystal plane spacing d is 0.34 nm.[33] Those are assigned to the graphite structure and CDs lattice spacing of (002) facet.[34]

In the (Fig. 2 a), the UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) of blue emission CDs exhibits the strong absorption peak centered at approximately 200 nm on account of π-π* transition of aromatic C = C bond. Besides, the typical peaks situated at 346 and 360 nm are individually attributed to n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 system double bond embodying C = O and C = N. The PL emission wavelength stimulated by 370 nm Xenon light source is centered at 450 nm while the PLQY of CDs reaches 26.4% under the same conditions, which reveals a small overlap with UV–vis spectrum, meaning that the fluorescence quenching of CDs is weak. The Fig. S3 presents the PL emission spectra of CDs as the excitation wavelengths increase sequentially from 300 to 390 nm. The emission peaks of CDs remain unchanged while there are changes on the excitation wavelengths, which means the emission of CDs presents excitation-independent characteristic. The time-solved PL (TRPL) spectra of the CDs implies the process of dynamic carrier recombination of CDs in the (Fig. 2b). The lifetime of the PL signal at 450 nm excited by 300 laser light sources is estimated as 11.56 ns fitted by double exponent decay function, which indicates the carriers in the CDs mainly participate in the radiation recombination leading to the good luminescence stability of the CDs.

Fig. 2c displays respectively the values of energy of the fermi edge and end edge, which is 16.02 eV for the fermi edge and 2.98 eV for the end edge. As illustrated in the (Fig. 2d), according to Kubelka-Munk theory, the abscissa axis is photon energy which is symbolized as hν and the longitudinal axis is (αhν)1/2 where α is the absorbance of the CDs in the band range of UV–vis. The bandgap of blue emission CDs can be obtained by the tangent method. The absorption edge of blue emission CDs is 450 nm with the value of the bandgap of 2.85 eV. The values of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) can be calculated through the results of ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). Ultimately, through the formula of - [21.22 eV - (16.02 – 2.98)] eV = − 8.18 eV (EVB = -[21.22–(Eendedge-Efermi)]) where 21.22 eV is the energy of He I, the position of HOMO can be acquired. Similarly, the position of LUMO can be obtained from the formula of - (8.18–2.85) eV = − 5.33 eV (ECB = EVB + Ebandgap). The bandgap transition of carriers in CDs is clearly emerged by UPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performances characterizations of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic structure of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs is illustrated in the (Fig. 3 a). The generally recognized structure is mainly composed of a patterned indium tin oxide (ITO) on the glass substrate as anode, poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) as hole injection layer (HIL), a few CDs doped in PVK, abbreviated as CDIP, as the emission layer, 1,3,5-tris(N-phenylbenzimidazol-2-yl) benzene (TPBi) as electron transport layer (ETL), 8-quinolinolato lithium (Liq) as electron injection layer (ETL) and aluminium as cathode. The Liq and Al make up a double layered cathode in order to improve charge transport between the interface of organic materials and metal electrode.

The energy-level diagram of materials corresponding to the schematic structure of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs is displayed in the (Fig. 3b). The energy levels of HOMO and LUMO are suitable for carrier transportation from electrodes to the emission layer since the energy levels for the injection of carriers are relatively weak. Therefore, holes in HOMO and electrons in LUMO which both can move freely can be primarily transmitted to the emission layer between the interface of HIL and active functional layer or ETL and active functional layer. Afterwards, the carriers are able to carry out radiation recombination at the emission layer and release energy in the form of EL. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) is applied to analyze the morphology and roughness of film surface. The AFM images of the films of pure PVK based LEDs (Fig. 3c) and CDs enhanced PVK LEDs (Fig. 3d) reveal both low roughness of the film surfaces. The root mean square roughness of the film of pure PVK based LEDs is 1.85 nm and the same for CDs enhanced PVK LEDs is 2.82 nm, which indicates the effects of both surface morphologies on charge transport are similar.

As shown in the (Fig. 4 a), a series of EL spectra of CDIP are applied to explore the EL performance of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs at various CDs-PVK mass ratios. The EL performance of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs is exhibited with distinct mass fractions of CDs and PVK ranging from 0 wt% to 4 wt%. Obviously, it achieves the optimum EL performance when the mass fraction of CDIP is 2 wt%. With the increase of mass of CDs, of which the range is from 0.5 wt% to 2 wt%, the EL intensity curves retain the same shape but the intensity of the peaks increased gradually. The peaks of EL are all situated at 432 nm that is close to the EL peak of PVK when the mass fraction of CDIP ranged from 0.5 wt% to 2 wt%, which means a trace of CDs could promote the light emission of PVK that is a kind of weakly luminescent CDMs mainly used for film-forming because of its excellent film forming property. Significantly, the EL peaks present the trend of redshift with the growth in quantity of CDs of which the range is from 2 wt% to 4 wt% as a result of the enhanced intensity of the EL peak of CDs in the whole EL spectrum. In the following discussion, we are going to mostly exhibit the performances of 2 wt% CDs enhanced PVK LEDs.

Fig. 4b displays that the EL spectra of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs with gradually increasing current densities, of which the scope is from 0.2 to 40 mA cm−2. As illustrated in the (Fig. 4e), the CIE coordinates of emission light of the devices are situated at (0.17, 0.10) and the picture of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs is inserted in the (Fig. 4e). The EL emission peak is centered at 432 nm whose location is very similar to that of pure PVK corresponding to the wavelength range of deep blue light. Moreover, the shape and the location of emission peaks almost remain unchanged but the EL intensity enhanced gradually with the increase of current density from 2.5 to 40 mA cm−2. The pure PVK based on LEDs are also fabricated in contrast to CDs enhanced PVK LEDs, of which the EL spectra and CIE coordinates are displayed in the (Fig. 4c and Fig. 4f) respectively. Pure PVK based LEDs own the EL wavelength of 432 nm and CIE coordinates of (0.22, 0.21). In the (Fig. 4f), the illustration is the photograph of illuminated pure PVK based LEDs. However, it is worth mentioning that large amounts of CDs cannot promote the effect of enhancing PVK luminescence. As emerged in the (Fig. 4d), the EL peaks move with the increase of the amounts of CDs, which indicates that carriers mainly recombine on the CDs and then EL of PVK evolve into the EL of CDs.

Fig. 5 presents the comparisons of EL performances between CDs enhanced PVK LEDs and pure PVK based LEDs. As emerged in the (Fig. 5a), the curves of power efficiency versus current density both decline rapidly at low current densities. However, when the current density increases from 2.5 to 10 mA cm−2, the power efficiency curve of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs (red line) possesses the smaller slope changes than those of the pure PVK based LEDs (black line), which indicates that CDs improve the mobility of carrier recombination between different interfaces of functional layers. In the (Fig. 5b), the curve of voltage versus current density illustrates that the turn-on voltage of the CDs enhanced PVK LEDs is 8.17 V while the turn-on voltage of pure PVK based LEDs is 9.91 V, which suggests CDs ameliorate the conductivity of the devices though the turn-on voltage of 8.17 V is still unsatisfied because of carriers primarily assembled on the PVK instead of CDs in these devices. Furthermore, the slope of the red line seems a constant, which implies the conductivity of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs is more stable. The curves of voltage versus luminance are displayed in the (Fig. 5c). It indicates that CDs enhanced PVK LEDs finally achieves the luminance of 176 cd m−2, which is 54.3% higher than that of pure PVK based LEDs whose luminance is 114 cd m−2, for the luminance area of 0.1 cm2. The curves in the (Fig. 5d) show that the highest current efficiency of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs (red line) is 0.5 cd A-1 while that of the pure PVK based LEDs (black line) is 0.48 cd A-1, implying that CDs slightly improve the current efficiency of the devices. In the (Fig. 5e), the half lifetimes of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs and pure PVK based LEDs are exhibited. The CDs enhanced PVK LEDs are proved to possess over 53 h half lifetime and the pure PVK based LEDs have 46 h half lifetime, which means CDs enhance the stable performance of devices to a certain extent. Ultimately, the CIE coordinates of various mass fractions of CDIP LEDs are calculated, which are presented in the (Fig. 5f). When the mass fractions of CDIP are 0 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 2.5 wt%, 3 wt% and 4 wt% respectively, the relevant CIE coordinates are situated at (0.22, 0.21), (0.19, 0.16), (0.18,0.13), (0.17, 0.10), (0.19, 0.17), (0.18, 0.16) and (0.18, 0.15), which shows CDs can effectively improve the color purity of EL referring to the CIE coordinates of pure blue light that are regulated as (0.13, 0.04) by ITU-R Recommendation BT.2020.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs enhancing EL of PVK
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in the (Fig. 6 a), the TRPL spectra of pure PVK, pure CDs and various mass fractions of CDIP are emerged respectively. The lifetime of pure PVK for PL decay is 19.77 ns and that of pure CDs is 11.58 ns which both adhere to double exponential decay. The curves of various mass fractions of CDIP all obey the double exponential decay as well. The lifetimes of various mass fractions of CDIP are 19.52, 17.89, 14.71, 14.28, 13.35, 12.12 ns separately, which is respectively assigned to the mass ratio of 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 2.5 wt%, 3 wt% and 4 wt% of CDIP. (Fig. 6b) presents the spot and line image of PL lifetimes of CDIP with various mass ratios. With the increase of amounts of CDs, the TRPL lifetimes of CDIP for PL decay gradually decline and all of the lifetimes are faster than that of pure PVK, which indicates CDs develop into the excimer polymer with PVK meanwhile accelerating the recombination of carriers.

The TPV test system is used to characterize and analyze the kinetic processes of photoelectrons extraction and interfacial charge transport in materials, of which the schematic diagram is shown in the (Fig. 6c).[35,36] A laser radiation is applied to inspire the electrons in samples whose wavelength is 355 nm and pulse width is 5 ns. By detecting the signals of photovoltage between two platinum electrodes, the process of charge transport in the samples can be reflected.[37] (Fig. 6d) illustrates the schematic diagram of charge transport on PVK molecules after carriers injection, which reflects that a part of carriers recombine on the PVK molecules to carry out EL while the other carriers waste because of charge transport between PVK molecules.

Fig. 7
a and (Fig. 7 b) emerge the TPV relaxation curves of pure PVK and 2 wt% CDIP separately. The TPV signal of pure PVK changes back and forth between positive and negative signals, which means excited electrons are transported randomly between PVK molecules because of the high conductivity of PVK. The TPV curve of 2 wt% CDIP displays the similar oscillation signal in the (Fig. 7 b). However, in the (Fig. 7 b), the frequency of variation between positive and negative signals is significantly reduced indicating that the addition of a small number of CDs efficiently reduced the charge transport between PVK molecules, which is instrumental in radiative recombination for carriers on the single PVK molecule so as to strengthen EL of PVK.

EMD is an adaptive time–space analysis method. EMD can locally counterbalance white noise and divide the signal sequences into a series of intrinsic mode functions (IMFs) in the case of retaining time information.[38–40] Among all the IMFs, the top-ranked IMFs correspond to the fast speed process with short periods and the low-ranked IMFs are assigned to the slow speed process with long periods.[41–44] Here, the TPV signals of pure PVK and 2 wt% doped in PVK are both divided into 10 IMFs through the method of EMD, among which the signals of IMF1, IMF3 and IMF7 are displayed in the (Fig. 7 c, Fig. 7 e and Fig. 7 f) respectively while other IMFs are all shown in the Fig. S4 .

Fig. 7 c exhibits the IMF1 of the TPV curves of pure PVK and 2 wt% CDIP transformed by EMD. For pure PVK, the signals are assembled on the range of 0.67 to 1.92 ms and 3.03 to 5.13 ms, which is corresponding to the processes of charge transmission between PVK molecules. As shown in the (Fig. 7 c), the IMF1 of 2 wt% CDIP own the more signal ranges and higher signal intensity in the time dimension, meaning that CDs increases the charge transport in the materials, which includes the process of electron transmission between not only PVK molecules but also CDs and PVK molecules. For the sake of exploring the effect of CDs on charge transport in detail, the frequency distribution is counted in the signals and converted into the corresponding time scale. (Fig. 7 d) presents the distributions the amounts of signals with different periods in the IMF1 signals of pure PVK and 2 wt% CDIP. As illustrated in the (Fig. 7 d), the range of different periods that are extracted from the EMD signals of pure PVK is from 0.03 to 0.11 ms, mainly focus on the 0.04 ms and 0.03 ms, which is ascribe to the charge transport between PVK molecules. However, with the addition of CDs, the range of different periods extracted from the EMD signals of 2 wt% CDIP is expanded, which is from 0.02 to 0.14 ms. Compared with signals of pure PVK, 0.02 ms take the important role in all periods, which is shorter than the main periods of pure PVK. This means that CDs improve the electron transport in the fast speed processes corresponding to the charge transport between CDs and PVK molecules, which is instrumental in EL of PVK. Besides the proportion of medium periods decrease and the relatively long periods such as 0.12 ms, 0.13 ms and 0.14 ms appear at the distribution of periods, which suggests CDs absorbed on the PVK molecules form some trap states that own the ability to bound or capture electrons. Similar conclusions can also be drawn from the distribution the amounts of signals with different periods in the IMF2 signals of pure PVK and 2 wt% CDIP shown in the Fig. S5 .

Moreover, in the (Fig. 7 e), the intensity of the signals of IMF3 of 2 wt% CDIP is much weaker than those of pure PVK. This means a considerable number of electrons in PVK instead of 2 wt% CDIP are involved in the medium speed process that is ascribe to charge transport between PVK molecules, which is the main path for carrier loss. Furthermore, in the (Fig. 7 f), the intensity of the signals of IMF7 of 2 wt% CDIP is much stronger than those of pure PVK, which means the addition of CDs increase the amounts of electrons participation in slow speed process that is related to the process of catalysis. In the field of catalysis, CDs are often used as modification materials of catalysts to extend the lifetime of electrons and holes, which is conducive to catalytic reactions that are assigned to slow speed process.[45–47] This is the characteristic of CDs that is primarily good for catalysis, which has been widely reported in the field of photocatalysis recently.[48–53] Eventually, according to our analysis, the schematic diagram of charge transport dynamics between CDs and PVK molecules is given out in the Fig. S6 . After 2 wt% CDIP, CDs reduce a portion of charge transport between PVK molecules that is adverse to EL and promote more carriers to recombine on the PVK molecules, thereby the EL of PVK can be enhanced.

The CDs are prepared by an ordinary hydrothermal method with the PLQY of 26.4% and the emission wavelength of 450 nm. We draw on the experience of the traditional structure of OLED to manufacture the CDs enhanced PVK LEDs. Notably, after doping the CDs into the PVK, the brightness of EL of PVK acquires significant improvement, which reaches 176 cd m−2 with the CIE coordinates situated at (0.17, 0.10). Table S1 summarized the several device performance parameters, including EL wavelength, half lifetime and luminance, of LEDs based on quantum dots, CDs and CPMs. CDs have quantities of advantages as one kind of auxiliary and functional materials.[54] Firstly, carbon materials are widely distributed and easily accessible on the earth.[55] Moreover, CDs is one kind of zero-dimensional quantum dot materials with non-toxicity and low cost.[33] The TPV test system is applied to analyze the kinetic process of charge transport in the materials.[56–59] The EMD is used to interpret TPV data to explain the effect of CDs on the charge transport kinetic characteristics in PVK for the first time. CDs may not represent the most excellent EL reinforcing materials, however, the CDs bring a brand-new understanding of the mechanisms of enhancement EL.

The CDs with the properties of enhancing EL of PVK are firstly fabricated by one step hydrothermal method. Then, the CDs are doped in PVK as the emission layer of LEDs to manufacture the CDs enhanced PVK LEDs with the EL wavelength of 432 nm. The brightness of 176 cd m−2 is 54.3% higher than that of pure PVK based LEDs and the CIE coordinates are situated at (0.17, 0.10). Furthermore, other performances of CDs enhanced PVK LEDs are all improved as well. TPV test and EMD method are used to analyze the effect of CDs on the charge transport kinetic characteristics in the complex materials of CDs and PVK. This work not only opens up a new way to improve the luminescence performance of EL devices, but also gives a new idea for further understanding the mechanism of CDs in improving the EL performances.

Tianyang Zhang: Data curation, Visualization. Xiao Wang: Formal analysis. Zhenyu Wu: Investigation, Software. Tianyu Yang: Software. Jiawei Wang: Software. Han Zhao: Investigation. Hui Huang: Writing – original draft. Yang Liu: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key R&D Program of China(2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101, 2020YFA0406103), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province(2019B010933001), National MCF Energy R&D Program(2018YFE0306105), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China(51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province(BK20190041), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.152649.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Kang, Organic Semiconductor/Carbon Dot Composites for Highly Efficient Hydrogen and Hydrogen Peroxide Coproduction from Water Photosplitting, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter., 13 (2021) 60561-60570.
